annotations.anchor_quote: HOW MULTIPOLARITY FUELS WAR (a). Multipolarity strengthens strategic uncertainty among great powers. · US-China - Russia competition. (b). Multipolarity sharpens the security dilemma and fuels arms race. · Indo-Pacific militarization
annotations.comment: Over-emphasis on war-fueling subpoints at expense of peace; balance with comparable detail on strategic measures.

annotations.anchor_quote: (a). Multipolarity strengthens strategic uncertainty among great powers. · US-China - Russia competition.
annotations.comment: Sub-points descriptive with examples only; add cause-effect analysis showing how they fuel war.

annotations.anchor_quote: By strengthening strategic uncertainty among great powers intensifying security dilemma and promoting bloc politics, multipolarity is inadvertently fuelling war.
annotations.comment: Missing commas after 'powers' and 'dilemma'; insert for parallel structure and readability.

annotations.anchor_quote: By strengthening strategic uncertainty among great powers intensifying security dilemma and promoting bloc politics, multipolarity is inadvertently fuelling war. Nevertheless, by empowering international institutions and prioritizing soft power, peace can be maintained ..
annotations.comment: Thesis relevant but lacks concise overview of main sections; add roadmap of war and peace arguments.

annotations.anchor_quote: (C), Multipolarity promotes alliances and counter alliances and instigates bloc politics .. · US-led and China-led blocs (d). Multipolarity undermines consensus building promoting institutional paralysis. · UN paralysis over Syria and Yemen (e). Multipolarity exacerbates the return of hard power.
annotations.comment: Excessive subpoints (c-e+) on war-fueling overemphasize one side; reduce to match peace measures' scope.

annotations.rubric_point: Flag if sub-points/arguments in the outline are merely descriptive and lack analytical depth.
annotations.anchor_quote: (d). Multipolarity undermines consensus building promoting institutional paralysis. · UN paralysis over Syria and Yemen
annotations.comment: Sub-points descriptively list examples without analysis; explain causal link to war promotion.

annotations.rubric_point: Flag if there is over-emphasis on one section at the expense of others.

annotations.anchor_quote: Multipolar systems are the most prone to war because they contain the greatest potential for miscalculations As the world is transitioning towards multipolarity, the risks of strategic miscalculation and great power contestation have surged.
annotations.comment: Introduction begins with quotes and lacks concise overview of main arguments/sections; add roadmap previewing war faultlines and peace measures after thesis.

annotations.anchor_quote: From Samuel P. Huntington's "Clash of Civilization" to Henry Kissinger's "World Order" and Fareed Zakaria's "Ten Lessons for the Post Pandemic World" all converge to say that multipolarity fuels war
annotations.comment: Specific book titles are unrelated details not essential to framing; remove or link explicitly to topic interpretation.

annotations.anchor_quote: samuel P. Huntington in his various essays on global order defines multipolarity " as "Multipolarity is af distribution of "power in which several major states or blocs possess comparable capabilities, preventing any single power from dominating the international system""
annotations.comment: Huntington quote provides definition but feels like unrelated background; tie explicitly to thesis on multipolarity fueling war.

annotations.anchor_quote: As Ronald Reagan notes in his book "An American Life" "Multipolarity intensifies the Thucydides Trap because several rising powers challenge several established ones simultaneously."
